runs.summary: 1 new idea, 1 new connection logged, 1 resolved
runs.content:
**Investigation idea:** Tim Ferriss's 'Fear-Setting' post sits un-ingested in raw/learning while its five sibling Ferriss pieces are already fully filed. raw/learning/Fear-Setting The Most Valuable Exercise I Do Every Month.md (tagged only 'clippings', not 'ingested') is the one Ferriss piece from tim.blog that never got a source page, unlike the five that did (self-help-trap, deloading, real-world-mba, ideal-lifestyle-costing, 17-questions). It's arguably the most actionable of the six — a structured worst-case/best-case/cost-of-inaction exercise that sits conceptually right next to learning/framework-decision-making.md (decisions ≠ outcomes) and learning/framework-opportunity-cost.md (cost of standing still), both already built out in the wiki. Next step: ingest into a new source-ferriss-fear-setting.md page and cross-link to both existing frameworks.

**Cross-domain connection:** Books' own recommender named the exact book that would fill Health's empty protocol page, five weeks ago, and nothing happened. books/suggestions.md's June 2026 entry for Outlive (Peter Attia) states outright that its four-pillar longevity framework 'maps directly to Fred's health protocol focus' — yet the book was never added to books/reading-list.md (still unread), and health/my-protocol.md remains 100% '[to be added]' placeholders across Supplements/Exercise/Diet/Biomarkers, unchanged since 2026-05-01. This is a sharper version of the already-logged Health-Goals-stub idea (2026-07-08, which pointed at the two ingested Wood/Godin source pages already in Health) — here the wiki's own recommendation engine, not just existing source material, already did the diagnostic work and pointed straight at the gap, and it's still sitting there unactioned.

**Resolved:** Technology domain's overview.md existence contradicted a stale memory/index.md note (originally logged 2026-07-14) — memory/index.md's Domain Content Status → Technology row now carries an explicit correction, added by the 2026-07-14 wiki-memory-update run. The older Open Threads bullet on the same topic wasn't touched in the same pass and still reads stale — a minor residual, not worth a fresh open item.

**Investigation idea:** Technology domain's overview.md already exists, contradicting a stale note in memory/index.md. technology/overview.md is a fully-built domain overview (8 sources, hyperscaler/semiconductor/enterprise-AI focus areas, updated 2026-07-05) — but memory/index.md's Domain Content Status and Open Threads sections still describe Technology as having 'no overview.md and no CLAUDE.md tag taxonomy,' a note dating to the 2026-06-07 index health check that was never reconciled after the overview page was created. Next step: flag for the next wiki-memory-update run to correct the Technology entries in Domain Content Status and Open Threads.

**Cross-domain connection 1:** Quantinuum's IPO and government-backed quantum buildout is the single largest unexamined technical risk to Fred's own Bitcoin holding. technology/source-morningstar-quantinuum-ipo-2026.md documents real capital and state backing accelerating quantum computing — $1.68B raised, $15.6B valuation, $100M from a Trump-administration quantum initiative (part of $2B+ committed to US quantum firms), Nvidia's VC arm as an investor, and QED Investors explicitly betting on quantum reaching large financial institutions within 5–10 years. Bitcoin's security model rests on ECDSA elliptic-curve signatures, which Shor's algorithm on a sufficiently large fault-tolerant quantum computer would break — the well-known 'Q-Day' risk to any address with an exposed public key. Fred holds BTC per Crypto portfolio.md, and Crypto - overview.md's 'Key Concepts to Build Out' list has six DeFi-mechanics items but zero mention of quantum risk. Not a near-term concern, but a tail risk currently invisible in the wiki's Bitcoin thesis.

**Cross-domain connection 2:** Salesforce's own Claude Code case study supplies the missing mechanism behind the Work-vs-AI&Tech deployment-speed contradiction flagged 2026-07-10. That earlier connection noted work/source-workday-disruption-debate-2026.md's a16z claim that AI-native competitors could 'deploy in 1 month' sits uncited against ai-tech/data-ai-enterprise-adoption-economist-2026-06-27.md's finding that 7–12 months is typical to ship an AI project. technology/source-salesforce-agentic-engineering-2026.md — not cited in that earlier connection — shows both can be true at once: Salesforce's headline 13-day, 18×-faster migration was only possible after institutionalising 'Claude Code Skills' and 'Foundation Plugins' (reusable, packaged engineering standards) as durable artifacts first — building that foundation is a separate, unmeasured phase that plausibly is the Economist's 7–12 months. The a16z '1 month' claim may only hold for firms that have already done the foundation-building work; for everyone else, the slow phase comes first.
